original_abstract:
The proliferation of large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) has established a paradigm where increasing model size consistently yields superior performance. However, as model scales reach hundreds of billions of parameters, conventional full-parameter fine-tuning becomes computationally and logistically prohibitive. This challenge has sparked the development of parameter-efficient adaptation techniques—methods that optimize only a small fraction of a model’s parameters while keeping the majority frozen. In this paper, we unify these diverse approaches under the framework of "delta-tuning," referring to the incremental changes (delta) applied to the pre-trained weights.

We provide a formal mathematical description of the delta-tuning problem and propose a comprehensive taxonomy that categorizes existing methods into addition-based, specification-based, and reparameterization-based approaches. Beyond empirical categorization, we offer theoretical insights into why delta-tuning is effective, interpreting it through the lenses of optimization in low-dimensional intrinsic subspaces and optimal control theory. To rigorously evaluate these methods, we conduct an extensive empirical study across more than 100 natural language processing tasks. Our analysis explores critical dimensions including performance parity with full fine-tuning, training convergence, computational efficiency, and the "power of scale," where we observe that larger backbone models become increasingly receptive to minimal parameter updates. We further investigate the combinability of different delta-tuning modules and their transferability across tasks. Our findings suggest that delta-tuning is not only a practical necessity for utilizing massive PLMs but also a theoretically sound strategy that reveals fundamental properties of how large-scale models capture and adapt knowledge.
humanized_abstract:
Over the last few years, huge pre-trained language models (PLMs) are shown consistently advancing state-of-the-art performance in almost all NLP benchmarks. The models have billions of parameters (in the range of hundreds of billions), and it is impractical to fine-tune them all during a downstream task. Thus, the parameter-efficient ways of model updating (also called as "adapting") have attracted significant interest. Most methods belong to one of two categories: learning spare new weights on top of frozen original PLM parameters (the first category, like few-shot learning or adapt learnt embedding); or computing small additional perturbations or deltas to be applied to the original, untouched (i.e. frozen) model parameters of the large pre-trained model (the second category). Based on the latter idea, we refer all the adaptation methods to the broader concept of delta-tuning.

In this work, we give a rigorous mathematical definition to the delta-tuning problem and give a taxonomy that, to the best of our knowledge, classifies all the previous works into three classes: addition-based, specification-based and reparameterization-based. While the previous works only give empirical observations to the delta-tuning problem, we provide some theoretical insights for the delta-tuning problem from two angles: optimization in the intrinsic low-dimensional subspace and optimal control theory. To validate our findings, we conduct experiments on over 100 NLP tasks to compare all the existing delta-tuning methods, covering various metrics: 1) the performance maintained with respect to the full fine-tuning (PT); 2) the training speed; 3) the time cost; and 4) the power of scale, which we find that larger backbones are often more tolerant of small updates. In addition, we examine the combination of the modules in the delta-tuning framework, as well as the potential for cross-task transferability. Our conclusion is that delta-tuning is necessary for the large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) and at the same time, it can also provide some new theoretical insights for us to understand how large-scale models learn and where they store the knowledge.

document.output:
Over the last few years, huge pre-trained language models (PLMs) are shown consistently advancing state-of-the-art performance in almost all NLP benchmarks. The models have billions of parameters (in the range of hundreds of billions), and it is impractical to fine-tune them all during a downstream task. Most methods belong to one of two categories: learning spare new weights on top of frozen original PLM parameters (the first category, like few-shot learning or adapt learnt embedding); or computing small additional perturbations or deltas to be applied to the original, untouched (i.e. frozen) model parameters of the large pre-trained model (the second category). In this work, we give a rigorous mathematical definition to the delta-tuning problem and give a taxonomy that, to the best of our knowledge, classifies all the previous works into three classes: addition-based, specification-based and reparameterization-based. document.input:
The proliferation of large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) has established a paradigm where increasing model size consistently yields superior performance.
